metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'release identity' — name the specific GitHub user, team, machine account, or App that will be allowed to push `refs/tags/v*` tags, so the Terraform `bypass_actors`/`allowed_actors` block can be written without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The 'release identity' that will be granted tag-push rights in the Terraform ruleset is never defined. The plan says 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never names what that identity is (a machine account, a GitHub App, the Gerrit deploy key, a specific org role, or an admin team).
findings.location: Scope — `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The release identity is the `gerrit-replication` deploy-key bot account (or the `release-bot` GitHub App — whichever is used for the Gerrit→GitHub mirror); the Terraform ruleset's `bypass_actors` block should reference that actor's node ID.' If the identity is TBD, pick a concrete default (e.g. a named admin team) and state it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'release identity' explicitly in the plan (or in a referenced operator runbook section): specify whether it is a GitHub user, team, GitHub App, or Actions bot, and how it maps to the existing Gerrit deploy key / CI identity — so the Terraform ruleset and the tagging step in `docs/releasing.md` name the same concrete principal.
findings.finding: The "release identity" — the GitHub actor that the Terraform ruleset grants tag create/update/delete rights to — is referenced in Scope, What, and AC but is never defined. It is a load-bearing cross-component coupling: the Terraform `main.tf` ruleset must name a concrete principal (user login, team slug, or app), and the human/automation that pushes tags must be that same principal, and the `authorize-source` workflow job must be reachable only via that identity's tag push. Without specifying what the identity is, the Terraform change and the workflow gate may be implemented inconsistently.
findings.location: Scope — `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` bullet; What — 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity'; AC — 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Release identity' subsection under What (or a table in `docs/releasing.md`) specifying e.g. 'The release identity is the GitHub team `org/release-managers`; the Terraform `bypass_actors` block will reference this team slug; human releasers must be members of this team to push a `v*` tag.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'the release identity' concretely in the plan — specify which actor (e.g., the human maintainer's GitHub account, a GitHub App, or a specific deploy key) receives `bypass_mode = always` for `refs/tags/v*`, and how that identity is provisioned.
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a Terraform ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` that restricts tag writes to 'the release identity', but never defines what that identity is. The existing `main.tf` bypass mechanism is a `DeployKey` with `bypass_mode = always` — that key is used for Gerrit replication, not for CI-triggered tag creation. GitHub Actions runners are not a DeployKey; they authenticate as the `GITHUB_TOKEN` installation. Without defining the bypass actor (e.g., a GitHub App, a specific service account, or an Actions environment token), the Terraform ruleset either locks out tag creation entirely or the implementation will have to guess the bypass identity, possibly defeating the security goal.
findings.location: What — fifth bullet; AC line 4 (`infra/terraform-github/main.tf` defines a ruleset over `refs/tags/v*`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and verify the concrete `actor_type`/`actor_id` (e.g., a GitHub App, a specific bot user, or a `RepositoryRole` bypass) that will serve as the tag-ruleset bypass for the 'release identity', confirm the Terraform GitHub provider >= 6.8.0 supports that bypass type, and update AC #4 and the `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` scope with the specific bypass design.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the tag ruleset (`refs/tags/v*`) in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` can restrict tag creation/update/deletion to a 'controlled release identity', but the existing `main.tf` explicitly documents (lines 23-28) that the tag_lock resource was REMOVED because there was no viable bypass actor for tag writes — the only supported bypass type currently is `DeployKey` (the Gerrit replication key). The plan never identifies what `actor_type`/`actor_id` would form the bypass for a 'release identity' (human tagger or GitHub Actions), leaving an unverified capability assertion at the heart of AC #4.
findings.location: What section — 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`'; AC #4; `infra/terraform-github/main.tf:23-28`
findings.suggested_fix: Consult the GitHub rulesets API and the `integrations/github` provider changelog to confirm whether an `actor_type = "Integration"` (GitHub Actions itself, integration_id=2) or a personal-access-token bypass exists. If no suitable bypass actor is available, the tag-ruleset AC should be scoped to only `deletion` + `non_fast_forward` rules (preventing tag deletion/force-push) while leaving tag creation open to the existing model, and the plan text should be updated to reflect this reduced constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC4 (add `refs/tags/v*` Terraform ruleset) with `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock`, which explicitly asserts this resource must NOT exist; the plan must either include updating that test (and the rationale for overriding the WS7 cutover decision) in its scope, or remove the Terraform change from scope.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 requires adding a `github_repository_ruleset` for `refs/tags/v*` in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, but `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` explicitly asserts that this resource MUST NOT exist (`assert 'resource "github_repository_ruleset" "tag_lock"' not in tf`, `assert 'target      = "tag"' not in tf`, `assert 'include = ["refs/tags/**"]' not in tf`). Implementing the plan as written will break a passing test, causing the plan's own final AC (`make test` passes) to fail.
findings.location: AC4 / infra/terraform-github/main.tf / tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` to allow a `tag_lock` ruleset when the bypass actor is a release identity (the WS7 comment in main.tf explicitly anticipated this: "To re-lock tags later, re-add it plus a release-tag path"); document the override rationale in the test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope note to AC4 (and the Scope section) that `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` in `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` must be updated (or a new complementary test added) to allow the `tag_lock` ruleset — otherwise AC4 and AC7 are mutually contradictory.
findings.finding: AC4 requires adding a `github_repository_ruleset` with `target = "tag"` to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, but the existing contract test `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` (tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46) explicitly asserts that `'resource "github_repository_ruleset" "tag_lock"' not in tf` and `'target = "tag"' not in tf`. Implementing AC4 without updating or superseding this test will cause `make test` to fail, directly contradicting AC7 ('make test passes').
findings.location: AC4 (infra/terraform-github/main.tf), tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and AC4, add: 'Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` — replace the `assert "tag_lock" not in tf` negative assertion with a positive contract test that pins the new tag ruleset's rules and bypass_actors, preserving the spirit of the contract (lock shape is auditable) while allowing the new resource.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the specific release identity (e.g., a named GitHub team, bot/service account, or deploy key) that will hold the ruleset bypass for `refs/tags/v*` in both the plan text and the AC for the Terraform change, so reviewers can verify the bypass list is minimal.
findings.finding: The plan requires a Terraform ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` that restricts tag creation/update/deletion to a 'controlled release identity', but never declares what that identity is (a specific GitHub team, bot account, service account, or deploy key). An implementer left to choose may grant bypass to all repository admins or all org members, recreating the exact gap the plan aims to close.
findings.location: Scope section / `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` bullet; AC: 'defines a ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope/What section and AC-4 naming the exact principal: e.g., 'The ruleset bypass list contains exactly one entry: the `release-bot` GitHub App / the `release` team with no admin members / the Gerrit replication deploy key — no individual user accounts and no admin-role bypass.' Reference this principal in the Terraform AC so the diff is reviewable against the stated constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what the 'release identity' is (actor type, actor ID, or mechanism — e.g., a GitHub Actions bot user, deploy key, or GitHub environment bypass) and specify the corresponding Terraform bypass_actors block shape for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`.
findings.finding: The 'release identity' referenced as the bypass actor for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` is never defined anywhere in the plan. The existing module only knows a single bypass actor type (DeployKey for Gerrit replication). The plan names no actor type, actor ID, or mechanism for what identity may create/update/delete `v*` tags — leaving the implementer without a specification for the core security primitive the AC depends on.
findings.location: What section ('Restrict refs/tags/v* creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in infra/terraform-github/main.tf') and AC4 ('restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Release identity' subsection under 'What' that names the actor: e.g., if a human maintainer will push tags, specify a named GitHub user bypass (`actor_type = "RepositoryRole"` or a specific user); if CI will push tags via the deploy key, confirm that the existing Gerrit deploy key is also the release key and document that; if the tag push stays manual with no additional Terraform enforcement (the gate being the workflow check instead), clarify that the Terraform ruleset is out of scope for this story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'release identity' for the `refs/tags/v*` ruleset bypass_actors (e.g., specify actor_type and actor_id, or call out that `docs/releasing.md` step 2's human `git push origin vX.Y.Z` must be replaced by a CI-driven tag push) before committing to this Terraform change.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 and What section specify 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never define what GitHub entity constitutes the 'release identity' — no actor_type (DeployKey, App, OrganizationAdmin, RepositoryRole?), actor_id, or GitHub identity is named. The `bypass_actors` block of a `github_repository_ruleset` requires concrete values; without them, the Terraform resource cannot be implemented, and a wrong choice could lock out the tag-push path entirely (replicating the same failure mode the existing main_lock comments warn about at length).
findings.location: What section ('Restrict refs/tags/v* creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity'), AC4 (infra/terraform-github/main.tf ruleset)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete 'release identity' that will be placed in the `refs/tags/v*` ruleset `bypass_actors` (e.g., specify `actor_type`, `actor_id`, and whether it is the same deploy key as `main`, a new CI-only deploy key, or a GitHub Actions environment identity).
findings.finding: The plan requires adding a `refs/tags/v*` Terraform ruleset 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never defines what entity 'the release identity' refers to. The existing `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` uses `actor_type = "DeployKey"` (the Gerrit replication key) for the `main` branch lock, but the tag-gate needs a different actor — presumably a GitHub Actions runner identity, a machine user, or a new deploy key — and no `bypass_actors` specification is provided anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section (bullet 5) and AC4: 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph to the What section specifying the bypass actor. Given the project's model (GitHub Actions OIDC for publishing, Gerrit deploy key for main), a dedicated tag-write deploy key (write-only, separate from the replication key) or a repository-scoped GitHub Actions bypass (`actor_type = "Integration"` pointing at the Actions app) are the natural choices; pick one and record it in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'release identity' bypass actor concretely in the plan (actor_type, actor_id or derivation method, bypass_mode) and add a precondition guard in the Terraform resource (analogous to the deploy-key existence check on `main_lock`) to abort `apply` if the actor is absent.
findings.finding: The 'release identity' that will be the bypass actor for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset is never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan says 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but does not specify what actor_type, actor_id, or bypass_mode this entails. Without this, implementers may grant an overly broad bypass (e.g., a RepositoryRole:admin or an entire team) that violates least-privilege, or accidentally replicate the open-tags status quo.
findings.location: What section (bullet 5); AC4: 'defines a ruleset over refs/tags/v* restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section and AC4, name the exact bypass actor — e.g., a dedicated GitHub Actions deploy key registered as 'rebar-release-tag-push' (`actor_type = "DeployKey", bypass_mode = "always"`), mirror the deploy-key existence precondition from `main_lock`, and add a runbook step to register the key before applying. Document the key's title in `variables.tf` analogously to `deploy_key_title`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `authorize-source` behaves on `workflow_dispatch` triggers (where there is no tag ref): either add an `if: github.event_name == 'push'` guard to `authorize-source`, or explicitly document that it short-circuits/skips the ancestry check on dispatch runs, so the dry-run path is not broken.
findings.finding: The plan quotes the publish-gate condition as `if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` but the actual code at release.yml lines 53, 81, and 116 reads `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')`. More importantly, the `build` job (lines 27-46) carries NO `if:` condition at all and runs on every trigger including `workflow_dispatch`. The plan's 'only gate anywhere' premise is thus slightly mis-stated: the real conditional on publishing jobs is compound (event_name + ref), and the build job is fully unconditioned. This does not invalidate the remediation, but the authorize-source job spec should account for the workflow_dispatch path (where `GITHUB_SHA` is the manually-dispatched SHA, not a tag) to avoid a false-pass or spurious failure on dry-run dispatches.
findings.location: Why section / release.yml:27-53
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` to the `authorize-source` job (matching the existing gate on publish/github_release/mcp_registry), and note in the AC that `workflow_dispatch` dry-runs intentionally skip source authorization (build only, no gate needed since no publication occurs).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan how `authorize-source` handles `workflow_dispatch` events: either skip the tag/version-lockstep checks (with `if: github.event_name == 'push'`) while still validating ancestor reachability, or document that dispatch dry-runs will skip `authorize-source` entirely via a conditional on the job.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the new `authorize-source` job should behave on `workflow_dispatch` events. `release.yml` currently accepts `workflow_dispatch` (line 21), and the dry-run path is described as a feature ('a manual run builds + validates the distributions but does NOT publish'). On a dispatch, there is no tag — the version-lockstep assertion (`tag_without_v == pyproject.toml version`) cannot be satisfied because `github.ref_name` is a branch name, not a version tag. The plan needs to scope this case explicitly.
findings.location: What section ('A source-authorization job that runs before any OIDC-enabled job') and AC2 ('version-lockstep step asserts tag_without_v … fails before build on any mismatch')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under the `authorize-source` job description: 'When `github.event_name == workflow_dispatch`, skip the tag-parse and version-lockstep assertions (no tag is being pushed); the ancestor-reachability check still runs against the dispatched branch HEAD, or the entire job is conditioned on `github.event_name == push`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `fetch-depth: 0` on the `actions/checkout` step in `authorize-source` so `git merge-base --is-ancestor` has the full ancestry graph; cite `reconcile-bridge.yml:88` as the existing precedent.
findings.finding: The `authorize-source` job's `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` check will fail spuriously (exit 1 = not an ancestor) when the checkout is shallow, because the shared ancestor commit is missing from the local clone. The existing `release.yml` does not set `fetch-depth`, defaulting to 1 (shallow). Every other ancestry-dependent workflow in the repo (`reconcile-bridge.yml:88`, `verify-identity.yaml:31`) explicitly uses `fetch-depth: 0` to avoid this. Without specifying `fetch-depth: 0` in `authorize-source`, the gate becomes unreliable — it will reject valid main-descended tags unless the ancestry chain happens to be within the shallow window.
findings.location: What — source-authorization job description; AC line 1 (`authorize-source` job)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `with: { fetch-depth: 0 }` to the `actions/checkout` step in the `authorize-source` job, and fetch `origin/main` explicitly (`git fetch origin main`) before running the ancestry check.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-before-apply discipline note and a rollback procedure for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset to `docs/releasing.md` or `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`, analogous to the existing main_lock rollback documented there.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify plan-before-apply discipline for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset change specifically. Applying the new ruleset without first reviewing a `terraform plan` output could immediately lock out the existing manual tag-push release flow (human push of `v*` tags) if the bypass actor is wrong or absent — a disruptive blast-radius event with no stated rollback path for the new resource.
findings.location: What section (bullet 5); Scope: 'infra/terraform-github/main.tf — a refs/tags/v* ruleset'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either manage the `pypi` environment protection rule via Terraform (using the `github_repository_environment` resource) so drift is caught by plan/apply, or add a periodic CI check (e.g., a scheduled workflow calling the GitHub Environments API) that fails loudly if required reviewers are absent.
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub environment's required-reviewers setting is shared mutable state that governs every production publication run, but the plan designates it as purely operator-attested with no machine-enforced ownership, drift-detection, or defined update/retire lifecycle — it can be silently removed by any org admin without triggering the plan's controls.
findings.location: AC line 6 — '[operator-attested] The live `pypi` GitHub environment has required reviewers enabled'; What section — 'production publication behind an independently-approved environment'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the `github_repository_environment` Terraform resource with `reviewers` and `deployment_branch_policy` blocks alongside the existing `main.tf` ruleset, so the environment reviewer requirement is code-reviewed, version-controlled, and drift-detected on every `terraform plan`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Dereference the annotated tag to its commit SHA before the ancestry check: use `git rev-parse "${GITHUB_REF}^{}"` (or `git rev-parse "refs/tags/${TAG}^{}"`) instead of bare `$GITHUB_SHA` in the `authorize-source` job.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the `authorize-source` job to use `$GITHUB_SHA` as the release commit SHA. On a tag-push event, `github.sha` / `$GITHUB_SHA` is set to the SHA of the *tag object* for annotated tags, not the underlying commit SHA. `git merge-base --is-ancestor` operates on commit objects only; passing a tag-object SHA causes git to error or silently misreport ancestry. The repo's release procedure in `docs/releasing.md` step 2 explicitly creates annotated tags (`git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m "nava-rebar X.Y.Z"`), so this is the live code path.
findings.location: What — source-authorization job description; `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`
findings.suggested_fix: In the `authorize-source` job, compute `RELEASE_SHA=$(git rev-parse "${GITHUB_REF}^{}")` (the `^{}` peel operator dereferences tag objects to commits) and use `$RELEASE_SHA` in the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ancestor-check vs exact-equality fork by stating a single default policy (recommended: `git merge-base --is-ancestor` for flexibility) and deleting the 'or' clause, so the executor implements one check without guessing.
findings.finding: The plan presents two mutually exclusive ancestor-check policies without resolving which one to implement: '`git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`, or exact-equality to the `main` tip if the project prefers'. The executor must choose between them with no stated default.
findings.location: What section — 'Prove the release SHA is reachable...' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: 'Prove the release SHA is reachable from reviewed `main`: `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` (exact-equality is intentionally excluded to support pre-merge tags).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a dedicated `docs/adr/release-source-authorization.md` / CLAUDE.md section) capturing the trust model: why ancestry-from-main is enforced, why both a Terraform tag-write ruleset AND a workflow ancestry check are needed, and the exact-equality vs. ancestor-reachable policy choice — referenced from `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a source-authorization gate enforcing that only commits reachable from reviewed `main` may trigger publication — but does not call for an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc section capturing the trust model, the rationale for exact-ancestry vs. equality policy, or the relationship between the Terraform ruleset and the workflow gate. `docs/releasing.md` is a runbook (how to release), not an architectural record (why the system is designed this way).
findings.location: Scope / `docs/releasing.md` bullet; AC last bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-release-source-authorization.md` with sections: Context (the open `refs/tags/v*` problem), Decision (ancestry gate + Terraform ruleset + environment approval), Consequences (operator must maintain release identity and required-reviewer env), and Alternatives considered (workflow_dispatch-only, tag signing). Reference it from `docs/releasing.md` intro.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `authorize-source` job script runs with `set -euo pipefail` (or equivalent) and that a failure of the `git fetch origin main` call itself causes the job to fail immediately, so the gate always fails closed on infrastructure errors.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify fail-safe behavior when the `git fetch origin main` step itself fails (e.g., network error, credential expiry). Since `authorize-source` is a security gate, a fetch failure that doesn't explicitly `exit 1` could allow the workflow to proceed or produce a misleading error, rather than cleanly blocking all downstream jobs.
findings.location: What section — authorize-source job, `git merge-base --is-ancestor` step; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC or implementation note: 'The authorize-source script must run under `set -euo pipefail`; any fetch/network/parse error exits non-zero and fails the job before merge-base is evaluated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deploy-ordering note specifying that the `refs/tags/v*` ruleset bypass actor must be validated in a staging/test repo before `terraform apply` runs in production, and that the Terraform change should be applied and verified working (a test tag push succeeds for the release identity, fails for others) before the `release.yml` workflow change is merged.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the sequencing between `terraform apply` (enforcing the `refs/tags/v*` ruleset) and merging the `release.yml` workflow changes. Applied out of order these produce a window of either broken releases (ruleset active, release identity bypass not confirmed) or incomplete protection (workflow gate present but tag restriction absent).
findings.location: Scope section — `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` and `.github/workflows/release.yml` changes; What section — 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/terraform-github/variables.tf` to the scope as an edit target and specify what new variable(s) the tag-ruleset resource will require (e.g., a release actor ID or actor type).
findings.finding: `infra/terraform-github/variables.tf` is an unenumerated edit target. Adding a new `github_repository_ruleset` resource for `refs/tags/v*` with a non-trivial bypass actor will almost certainly require a new input variable (e.g., a release identity actor ID or actor type). The plan's scope lists only `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` but not `variables.tf`.
findings.location: Scope section: 'infra/terraform-github/main.tf — a refs/tags/v* ruleset restricting tag writes to the release identity'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a minimum `permissions:` block for the `authorize-source` job in the plan (e.g., `contents: read`, no write grants) and add it as a verifiable AC condition.
findings.finding: The new `authorize-source` job performs privileged git operations (fetching the protected `origin/main` ref) on the boundary-crossing release path, but the plan does not specify a minimum `permissions:` block for that job. In GitHub Actions, if the workflow-level permissions are set to write-all (a common default for release workflows that create GitHub Releases), the `authorize-source` job inherits write access to contents, packages, and deployments — more than needed for a read-only gate job that should only need `contents: read`.
findings.location: What section / `.github/workflows/release.yml` — new `authorize-source` job
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The `authorize-source` job declares `permissions: contents: read` (no write grants); the `publish` job declares `permissions: id-token: write, contents: read` for OIDC only.' This scopes each job to its actual needs and is verifiable in the resulting diff.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ancestor-check policy to a single algorithm (is-ancestor OR exact-equality) and update AC1's verification criteria to reflect the chosen policy unambiguously.
findings.finding: The `authorize-source` job's core check is specified as two mutually exclusive algorithms with no resolution: '`git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`, or exact-equality to the `main` tip if the project prefers.' These produce meaningfully different security properties (is-ancestor allows any merged commit; exact-equality requires the tag to be on the HEAD of main), yet the plan defers the choice to the implementer with 'if the project prefers'.
findings.location: What section, bullet 2: 'Prove the release SHA is reachable from reviewed `main` per policy (`git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`, or exact-equality to the `main` tip if the project prefers).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
